event: The Apollo 11 Cave in the Huns Mountains is later found to hold some of the oldest known figurative rock art in southern Africa, dating to around this period.
San (Bushmen) hunter-gatherer communities, ancestors of the region's earliest inhabitants, range across the arid landscape, leaving extensive rock engravings such as those at Twyfelfontein.
Khoikhoi pastoralists, including the Nama, begin migrating into the region with herds of cattle and sheep, introducing herding to the area.
Bantu-speaking peoples, including the Ovambo and Herero, migrate into northern and central Namibia during the wider Bantu expansion, establishing cattle-herding and agricultural societies.
Portuguese explorer Diogo Cao reaches the Namibian coast and erects a stone cross (padrao) at Cape Cross.
Portuguese navigator Bartolomeu Dias lands near present-day Luderitz, naming the bay Angra Pequena.
The Dutch authorities at the Cape annex Walvis Bay to forestall rival European claims to the strategic natural harbour.
Britain seizes the Cape Colony from the Dutch and with it asserts interest in Walvis Bay.
German and Rhenish missionaries begin establishing Christian mission stations among the Nama and Herero.
Jonker Afrikaner, an Oorlam-Nama leader, consolidates power at Windhoek and dominates trade and conflict in central Namibia.
A fragile peace agreement is brokered at Okahandja between the Herero and Nama after decades of intermittent warfare.
Britain formally annexes Walvis Bay, attaching the enclave to the Cape Colony.
German trader Adolf Luderitz purchases land around Angra Pequena, laying the basis for a German colonial foothold.
Germany declares a protectorate over the territory, founding the colony of German South West Africa during the Scramble for Africa.
The Berlin Conference's partition of Africa confirms German control over South West Africa.
The Heligoland-Zanzibar Treaty between Britain and Germany defines the Caprivi Strip, granting Germany access toward the Zambezi River.
Diamonds and copper prospects spur German settlement and the growth of towns such as Windhoek and Swakopmund.
The Herero, led by Samuel Maharero, rise in revolt against German rule, soon joined by the Nama under Hendrik Witbooi.
German forces under General Lothar von Trotha carry out the Herero and Nama genocide, killing tens of thousands through battle, starvation, and concentration camps.
The discovery of diamonds near Luderitz triggers a mining boom and the creation of restricted prospecting zones (Sperrgebiet).
South African forces invade and conquer German South West Africa during World War I, ending German colonial rule.
The League of Nations grants South Africa a Class C mandate to administer South West Africa.
South Africa crushes the Bondelswarts Nama uprising with aircraft and machine guns, drawing international criticism.
The newly formed United Nations refuses South Africa's request to annex South West Africa and calls for it to be placed under trusteeship.
South Africa's National Party victory leads to the extension of apartheid laws and policies to South West Africa.
Sam Nujoma and others found the South West Africa People's Organisation (SWAPO) to fight for independence.
The UN General Assembly revokes South Africa's mandate, and SWAPO launches its armed struggle with an attack at Omugulugwombashe.
The UN General Assembly renames the territory Namibia, asserting direct UN responsibility for its administration.
The International Court of Justice rules that South Africa's continued presence in Namibia is illegal.
The UN recognizes SWAPO as the sole legitimate representative of the Namibian people.
South Africa convenes the Turnhalle Conference to promote an internal settlement opposed by SWAPO and the UN.
The UN Security Council adopts Resolution 435, setting out a plan for Namibian independence through UN-supervised elections.
South Africa installs a Transitional Government of National Unity in Windhoek, which fails to win international recognition.
The Tripartite Accord, linking Cuban troop withdrawal from Angola to Namibian independence, paves the way for implementing Resolution 435.
UN-supervised elections to a Constituent Assembly are held, with SWAPO winning a majority as the transition to independence begins.
Namibia gains independence on 21 March with Sam Nujoma as its first president, and joins the United Nations.
Namibia becomes a member of the Organisation of African Unity, the forerunner of the African Union.
South Africa hands over the Walvis Bay enclave and offshore islands to Namibian sovereignty.
Sam Nujoma is re-elected president as SWAPO wins a sweeping majority in national elections.
A short-lived secessionist uprising in the Caprivi Strip is swiftly suppressed by Namibian security forces.
Namibia becomes a founding member of the African Union when it succeeds the Organisation of African Unity.
Hifikepunye Pohamba succeeds Sam Nujoma as president after winning the 2004 elections.
Hage Geingob is inaugurated as president following his decisive election victory.
Germany officially recognizes the colonial-era killings of the Herero and Nama as genocide and pledges reconstruction aid.
President Hage Geingob dies in office in February and is succeeded by Vice-President Nangolo Mbumba.
Netumbo Nandi-Ndaitwah is inaugurated as Namibia's first female president after winning the 2024 election.
